parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin dis-, here suggesting an unfolding or spreading out — not reversing something, but opening it up. Think of 'de-' as pulling something apart from its envelope.
From Old French voloper/envelopper (to wrap). The same root appears in 'envelope.' So développer literally means to unwrap or unfold.
A phonetic extension of the root, part of the verb stem développer. Not a separate meaningful morpheme — just part of how the verb is built.

words.exampleSentence: Le développement de cette région a été rapide ces dernières années.
words.exampleTranslation: The development of this region has been rapid in recent years.
words.flag: 5+ parts — velop/pe boundary is phonetic rather than clean morpheme split

parts.homeLangDetails: A variant of in- (not) that appears before words starting with p or b, following standard Latin/French sound rules. Same meaning as in- — pure negation.
From Latin ponere (to place, to put). The same root appears in 'position,' 'possible,' and 'imposer.' Something 'possible' is literally something that can be placed or done.

words.exampleSentence: Il est impossible de finir ce travail en une heure.
words.exampleTranslation: It is impossible to finish this work in one hour.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Greek organon (tool, instrument). Used here in the sense of organizing or giving structure — the same root as 'organize' and 'organism.'
Part of the French verbalizing suffix -iser (equivalent to English -ize). Together with -er below, it turns the noun root into a verb.

words.exampleSentence: Nous devons réorganiser notre emploi du temps.
words.exampleTranslation: We need to reorganize our schedule.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin ferre (to carry, to bear). The idea behind 'prefer' is to carry one thing before another — to place it ahead. The same root appears in 'conférence,' 'référence,' and 'transfer.'

words.exampleSentence: Elle a une préférence pour la musique classique.
words.exampleTranslation: She has a preference for classical music.

words.exampleSentence: Quelle est ta nationalité ?
words.exampleTranslation: What is your nationality?

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin com- (together, fully). In 'comprendre' it acts as an intensifier — grasping something fully or completely. The same prefix appears in 'complet' and 'combiner.'
From Latin prehendere (to grasp, to seize). The core idea of 'comprehend' is to mentally grasp something. The same root appears in 'appréhender' and English 'prehensile.'

words.exampleSentence: Son comportement est totalement incompréhensible.
words.exampleTranslation: His behavior is totally incomprehensible.

words.translationLong: to surpass, to exceed, to outdo
words.exampleSentence: Elle a réussi à surpasser tous ses concurrents.
words.exampleTranslation: She managed to surpass all her competitors.

parts.homeLangDetails: A French prefix reversing or negating the base word. Before vowels it appears as dés-, as here. It directly mirrors English 'dis-' in words like 'disagreeable.'
From Latin gratus (pleasing, welcome). The same root gives us 'agrément' (pleasure, charm) and English 'agreeable.' It carries the sense of something welcome or pleasant.

words.exampleSentence: Ce temps froid et pluvieux est vraiment désagréable.
words.exampleTranslation: This cold and rainy weather is truly unpleasant.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Old French aprendre (to learn), itself from Latin apprehendere (to grasp, to take hold of). The root captures the idea of grasping knowledge. It gives us both 'apprendre' (to learn) and 'apprenti' (apprentice).

An infix that appears in French verbs derived from certain patterns (like the -issons, -issez forms of -ir verbs), and is carried into noun derivatives. It extends the stem without adding independent meaning.

words.exampleSentence: L'apprentissage d'une nouvelle langue demande du temps et de la patience.
words.exampleTranslation: Learning a new language takes time and patience.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin struere (to build, to pile up, to arrange). The same root appears in 'structure,' 'destruction,' 'instruction,' and 'infrastructure.' The core idea is deliberate arrangement or building.

words.exampleSentence: La construction du nouveau pont prendra deux ans.
words.exampleTranslation: The construction of the new bridge will take two years.

words.exampleSentence: La transformation de cette vieille usine en appartements est impressionnante.
words.exampleTranslation: The transformation of this old factory into apartments is impressive.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin videre/visus (to see, sight). The same root appears in 'visible,' 'vision,' 'visuel,' and English 'visual.' It always relates to seeing or being seen.
The Latin-origin form of the -able suffix. In 'visible,' it means 'capable of being seen.' When combined with -ité, the full sequence -ibilité mirrors English -ibility (as in visibility, legibility).

words.exampleSentence: La visibilité est très réduite à cause du brouillard.
words.exampleTranslation: Visibility is very limited because of the fog.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin colligere (to gather together): col- (together) + legere (to gather, to pick). The same root gives us 'collection,' 'collectif,' and 'colleague' (one gathered with you).

words.exampleSentence: Nous avons décidé collectivement de changer notre approche.
words.exampleTranslation: We collectively decided to change our approach.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here from Latin ante- (before, in advance), not from Greek anti- (against). In 'anticiper,' it means to act before something happens — to get ahead of it. Worth noting because the same letters in other words (like 'antibiotique') do mean 'against.'

From Latin capere (to take, to seize). The same root appears in 'participer' (to take part), 'précipit-' (headlong), and English 'capture.' Here the idea is to seize or grasp something before it arrives.

words.translationLong: to anticipate, to foresee, to expect
words.exampleSentence: Il faut anticiper les problèmes avant qu'ils surviennent.
words.exampleTranslation: We must anticipate problems before they arise.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here dé- reverses or undoes the composition — it breaks something into its component parts. This is the undoing sense of dé-, directly parallel to English 'de-' in 'decompose.'
From Latin ponere (to place). Together with com-, 'composer' means to put things together. So 'décomposer' is to undo that putting-together — to take apart what was assembled.

words.translationLong: to decompose, to break down, to take apart
words.exampleSentence: On peut décomposer ce problème en plusieurs étapes simples.
words.exampleTranslation: We can break this problem down into several simple steps.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin realis, relating to 'res' (thing/reality). The root that gives us 'real' and 'reality' in English too.
This is the verbalizing element that turns the adjective root into a verb (réaliser = to realize/achieve). It corresponds to the English '-ize' suffix.

words.exampleSentence: La réalisation de ce projet a pris deux ans.
words.exampleTranslation: The completion of this project took two years.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin progredi (pro- = forward + gradi = to step). The same root as English 'progress' — moving forward step by step.
The French adjectival suffix -if/-ive corresponds directly to English '-ive', turning a noun into an adjective meaning 'having the quality of'.

words.exampleSentence: Le changement climatique nécessite une réponse progressive.
words.exampleTranslation: Climate change requires a progressive response.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin dependere, meaning 'to hang down from' (de- = down/from + pendere = to hang). So 'depend' literally means to hang from something — to rely on it as a support. The de- here is directional, not reversing.

words.exampleSentence: Le pays a proclamé son indépendance en 1960.
words.exampleTranslation: The country proclaimed its independence in 1960.

words.exampleSentence: Il a accompli un effort surhumain pour terminer la course.
words.exampleTranslation: He made a superhuman effort to finish the race.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin parare, meaning 'to make ready, to arrange, to provide'. The same root appears in 'prepare' in English, and in words like 'repair' and 'separate'.

words.exampleSentence: La préparation du repas a duré une heure.
words.exampleTranslation: The preparation of the meal took an hour.

words.exampleSentence: Elle ne pouvait pas tolérer cette injustice.
words.exampleTranslation: She could not tolerate this injustice.

parts.homeLangDetails: The French verbalizing suffix -iser corresponds directly to English '-ize', meaning 'to make or become'. Here: to make modern.

words.translationLong: to modernize, to update, to bring up to date
words.exampleSentence: Le gouvernement veut moderniser le réseau ferroviaire.
words.exampleTranslation: The government wants to modernize the rail network.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin flectere/flexum, meaning 'to bend'. The same root gives us English 'flex', 'flexible', and 'reflex'.
From Latin -ibilis, the same root as the French -ible and English '-ible/-able' suffix, meaning 'capable of being'. So flexibil- = capable of being bent.

words.exampleSentence: Ce poste exige une grande flexibilité horaire.
words.exampleTranslation: This position requires great schedule flexibility.

parts.homeLangDetails: The French prefix dés- (also written dé- before consonants) corresponds to English 'dis-' or 'un-', reversing or negating the base word. Here it turns 'ordre' (order) into its opposite.

words.exampleSentence: Sa chambre était dans un désordre total.
words.exampleTranslation: His room was in total disorder.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin construere (con- = together + struere = to pile/build). The same root as English 'construct', 'structure', and 'infrastructure'.

words.exampleSentence: La reconstruction du pays après la guerre a pris des décennies.
words.exampleTranslation: The reconstruction of the country after the war took decades.

words.exampleSentence: Elle travaille pour une organisation internationale.
words.exampleTranslation: She works for an international organization.

parts.homeLangDetails: A variant of the prefix in- (not-) used before words beginning with 'p' or 'b' for ease of pronunciation. Exactly as in English 'impatient', 'impossible', 'imperfect'.

words.exampleSentence: Les enfants étaient impatients d'ouvrir leurs cadeaux.
words.exampleTranslation: The children were impatient to open their gifts.

words.translationLong: to foresee, to anticipate, to plan, to forecast
words.exampleSentence: Il est difficile de prévoir le temps qu'il fera demain.
words.exampleTranslation: It is difficult to predict the weather tomorrow.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin sentire, meaning 'to feel, to perceive'. The same root appears in English 'sense', 'sensitive', and 'sentiment'.
From Latin -ibilis, meaning 'capable of being'. So sensibil- = capable of being felt or perceived.

words.exampleSentence: Elle a une grande sensibilité artistique.
words.exampleTranslation: She has a great artistic sensitivity.

parts.homeLangDetails: The stem of 'racisme' (racism), from 'race' + the suffix -isme. Broken here at the point where -me joins the word as a noun suffix.
The French suffix -isme corresponds exactly to English '-ism', forming nouns that denote a belief, movement, or ideology. Here the full word 'racisme' is embedded inside 'antiracisme'.

words.exampleSentence: L'antiracisme est au cœur de leur engagement associatif.
words.exampleTranslation: Antiracism is at the heart of their community involvement.

words.exampleSentence: Le formulaire est incomplet.
words.exampleTranslation: The form is incomplete.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the verb douer (to endow with a gift), related to don (gift). So surdoué is literally 'over-gifted' — endowed with exceptional natural ability.

words.exampleSentence: Cet enfant surdoué a appris à lire à l'âge de trois ans.
words.exampleTranslation: This highly gifted child learned to read at the age of three.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here dé- signals removal or departure from a place — the opposite of emménager (to move in). Think of it as 'un-settling' from a home.
From ménage (household, home). The same root appears in ménager (to manage carefully) and ménagère (housewife/homemaker).

words.exampleSentence: Nous allons déménager dans une nouvelle ville le mois prochain.
words.exampleTranslation: We are going to move to a new city next month.

parts.homeLangDetails: From apparaître (to appear), itself from Latin apparere. The -aît- spelling is the stem in conjugated forms, but the infinitive base apparaît- is used here before the -re ending.
The infinitive ending for -re verbs in French. Not to be confused with the prefix ré- at the start of the word.

words.exampleSentence: Le soleil a réapparu après l'orage.
words.exampleTranslation: The sun reappeared after the storm.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin producere (to lead forward, to bring forth). The same root gives us produire, produit, and production in French.
An adjectival suffix meaning 'having the quality of' — turning the noun root into productif/productive (productive).
A nominalizing suffix equivalent to English -ity, turning an adjective into an abstract noun. Productive → productivity, actif → activité, etc.

words.exampleSentence: La productivité de l'équipe a augmenté grâce au nouveau logiciel.
words.exampleTranslation: The team's productivity increased thanks to the new software.

parts.homeLangDetails: From information, shortened to its root. Informatique was coined in French in the 1960s by blending information + automatique, so this part carries the 'information' half of that blend.
A suffix forming adjectives or nouns related to a field or quality — like English -ic or -ics. Mathématique, physique, and informatique all use this pattern to denote a discipline.

words.exampleSentence: Elle fait des études en informatique à l'université.
words.exampleTranslation: She is studying computer science at the university.

parts.homeLangDetails: The standard French adverbial suffix, equivalent to English -ly. It attaches to the feminine form of the adjective — so indirecte becomes indirectement.

words.exampleSentence: Il a indirectement causé le problème en ignorant les avertissements.
words.exampleTranslation: He indirectly caused the problem by ignoring the warnings.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin praeferre (to carry before, to favor). The same root gives us préférer and préférence. The accent on é marks the French adaptation of the Latin prae- prefix.

words.exampleSentence: Il serait préférable d'arriver en avance.
words.exampleTranslation: It would be preferable to arrive early.

parts.homeLangDetails: A prefix expressing negation or reversal, equivalent to English dis-. Before a vowel it appears as dés-. The same prefix appears in désaccord (disagreement) and désordre (disorder).

words.exampleSentence: Le principal désavantage de ce plan est son coût élevé.
words.exampleTranslation: The main disadvantage of this plan is its high cost.

words.exampleSentence: La transformation de la ville au cours des dernières années est impressionnante.
words.exampleTranslation: The transformation of the city over the last few years is impressive.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin respondere (to answer, to pledge in return). The root carries the idea of being answerable or accountable — which is the core of responsibility.
A Latin-origin suffix meaning 'capable of' or 'able to be.' Here it's in its Latin form -abil- (rather than French -able) because it's sandwiched between the root and the noun suffix.
A nominalizing suffix equivalent to English -ity, turning the adjectival stem into an abstract noun: responsable → responsabilité.

words.exampleSentence: Elle a pris la responsabilité de gérer le projet.
words.exampleTranslation: She took on the responsibility of managing the project.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin mons/montis (mountain). The same root appears in montagne (mountain), montée (ascent), and remonter (to go back up). Surmonter literally means 'to climb over' something.

words.translationLong: to overcome, to surmount, to get over
words.exampleSentence: Il a réussi à surmonter ses peurs grâce à beaucoup d'efforts.
words.exampleTranslation: He managed to overcome his fears through a lot of effort.

parts.homeLangDetails: From obéir (to obey), from Latin oboedire. The stem shortens to obé- when the suffix is added. The full verb obéir (to obey) is the direct opposite of désobéir.
The infinitive ending for -ir verbs in French.

words.exampleSentence: Il est dangereux de désobéir aux règles de sécurité.
words.exampleTranslation: It is dangerous to disobey safety rules.

parts.homeLangDetails: The full verb utiliser (to use, to utilize), from Latin utilis (useful). It is treated here as a single root that re- is prefixed onto, rather than being broken down further into util + iser.

words.exampleSentence: On peut réutiliser ces sacs en tissu pour faire ses courses.
words.exampleTranslation: You can reuse these fabric bags for grocery shopping.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin comprehendere (to grasp, to take in fully). The same root gives us comprendre (to understand) in French and comprehension in English. The -s- here is part of the Latin stem that surfaces in noun forms.

words.exampleSentence: Son incompréhension du sujet était évidente lors de la réunion.
words.exampleTranslation: His lack of understanding of the subject was evident during the meeting.

words.exampleSentence: Elle est très perfectionniste dans son travail.
words.exampleTranslation: She is very much a perfectionist in her work.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin de-, here meaning 'down' or 'pressing down' — not reversing an action but describing a downward force on mood or spirit. The same sense lives on in English 'depression.'

Here -eur functions as a nominalizing suffix forming a noun from the verb déprimer, identifying the substance that acts against depression. It's the French equivalent of the English agentive suffix -ant or -er, as in 'suppressant.'

words.exampleSentence: Son médecin lui a prescrit un antidépresseur.
words.exampleTranslation: Her doctor prescribed her an antidepressant.

words.exampleSentence: La surpopulation est un défi majeur pour les grandes villes.
words.exampleTranslation: Overpopulation is a major challenge for large cities.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here dé- reverses or removes the state described by the root — stripping away courage, just as English 'dis-' does in 'discourage.' This is the reversing sense of dé-, not the 'down from' sense seen in words like dépresseur.
The -er ending marks the infinitive in French. The final -r here represents that ending after the stem already ends in -e.

words.exampleSentence: Les obstacles ne doivent pas te décourager.
words.exampleTranslation: Obstacles should not discourage you.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin in-, here meaning 'into' or 'onto' — the sense is of coming upon something or finding something within. It's the same prefix in English 'invent.'
From Latin venire (to come). The same root appears in English 'convention,' 'adventure,' and 'event.' In inventer, the idea is of 'coming upon' a new idea.

words.exampleSentence: L'entreprise doit se réinventer pour rester compétitive.
words.exampleTranslation: The company must reinvent itself to stay competitive.

words.exampleSentence: On a découvert des peintures préhistoriques dans cette grotte.
words.exampleTranslation: Prehistoric paintings were discovered in this cave.

words.exampleSentence: Il agissait inconsciemment, sans réaliser ce qu'il faisait.
words.exampleTranslation: He was acting unconsciously, without realizing what he was doing.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here dés- strips away the state of illusion — removing a false belief. This is the reversing or undoing sense of dé-/dés-, closely parallel to English 'dis-' in 'disillusion.'

From Latin illudere (to mock, to play upon), related to ludere (to play). An illusion is something that 'plays tricks' on your perception. The same root appears in 'illusion,' 'elusive,' and 'delude.'

words.exampleSentence: La désillusion était grande après tant d'espoir.
words.exampleTranslation: The disillusionment was profound after so much hope.

words.exampleSentence: Elle travaille pour une organisation internationale.
words.exampleTranslation: She works for an international organization.

parts.homeLangDetails: The French verbalizing suffix -iser, equivalent to English -ize. It turns the adjective moderne into the verb moderniser (to modernize). Here only the stem -is- appears, with the noun suffix -ation following.

words.exampleSentence: La modernisation des infrastructures est en cours.
words.exampleTranslation: The modernization of infrastructure is underway.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin permeare (to pass through), combining per- (through) + meare (to go, to pass). Something perméable lets things pass through it freely — water, air, ideas.

words.exampleSentence: N'oublie pas ton imperméable, il va pleuvoir.
words.exampleTranslation: Don't forget your raincoat, it's going to rain.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here pré- conveys the idea of something occupying the mind beforehand or in advance — a concern that takes hold of your thoughts before other things can. Think of it as your worry 'pre-occupying' the mental space.

words.exampleSentence: Sa principale préoccupation est la santé de ses enfants.
words.exampleTranslation: Her main concern is her children's health.

words.exampleSentence: Il ne faut pas surcharger le programme de la semaine.
words.exampleTranslation: You shouldn't overload the week's schedule.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here dé- signals a reduction or lowering — the opposite of increasing value. It's the same directional-downward sense as in English 'devalue' or 'deflate.'

words.exampleSentence: La dévaluation de la monnaie a affecté l'économie du pays.
words.exampleTranslation: The devaluation of the currency affected the country's economy.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Greek organon (tool, instrument). In reorganisation, the core idea is of arranging the 'working parts' of something — a company, a system — into a functioning whole. The same root gives us 'organism' and 'organize.'
The French verbalizing suffix -iser (equivalent to English -ize), here reduced to its stem -is- before the noun ending -ation is added.

words.exampleSentence: Une réorganisation interne a été annoncée par la direction.
words.exampleTranslation: An internal reorganization was announced by management.

words.exampleSentence: Le budget alloué est insuffisant pour couvrir tous les frais.
words.exampleTranslation: The allocated budget is insufficient to cover all expenses.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here dé- reverses or undoes the idea of setting something in place — to de-termine is to un-fix or undo a boundary. Combined with pré-, the whole word means to fix something in advance.

words.translationLong: to predetermine, to decide in advance
words.exampleSentence: Certaines théories affirment que le destin est prédéterminé.
words.exampleTranslation: Some theories claim that fate is predetermined.
words.flag: note: dé- + termin- could arguably be one root 'déterminer'; split reflects morpheme transparency

words.exampleSentence: Son désenchantement face à la politique était évident.
words.exampleTranslation: His disenchantment with politics was evident.

words.exampleSentence: Le serveur était surchargé et le site ne répondait plus.
words.exampleTranslation: The server was overloaded and the site was no longer responding.

words.exampleSentence: Ces deux logiciels sont incompatibles entre eux.
words.exampleTranslation: These two pieces of software are incompatible with each other.

parts.homeLangDetails: This is the Latin nominal suffix -ura, absorbed into French as part of the root form 'structure'. It turns the verb root struct- into a noun meaning 'a building' or 'an arrangement'.

words.exampleSentence: La restructuration de l'entreprise a entraîné des licenciements.
words.exampleTranslation: The restructuring of the company led to layoffs.

words.exampleSentence: La préservation de la biodiversité est un enjeu mondial.
words.exampleTranslation: The preservation of biodiversity is a global issue.

words.exampleSentence: Aucun être humain n'est parfait — nous sommes tous imparfaits.
words.exampleTranslation: No human being is perfect — we are all imperfect.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -iser (equivalent to English -ize) turns a noun or adjective into a verb. Here it creates 'to de-moralize' from 'moral'. The -er ending is the standard French infinitive ending that follows.

words.translationLong: to demoralize, to discourage, to dishearten
words.exampleSentence: Les défaites répétées ont fini par démoraliser l'équipe.
words.exampleTranslation: The repeated defeats eventually demoralized the team.

words.translationLong: to reintegrate, to reinstate, to rejoin
words.exampleSentence: Il a été difficile pour elle de réintégrer le marché du travail.
words.exampleTranslation: It was difficult for her to reintegrate into the job market.

words.exampleSentence: La situation était tellement bizarre qu'elle en devenait surréaliste.
words.exampleTranslation: The situation was so strange that it was becoming surreal.

words.exampleSentence: Ce comportement est totalement inacceptable en milieu professionnel.
words.exampleTranslation: This behavior is completely unacceptable in a professional setting.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here -é functions both as a past participle ending (literally 'pre-judged') and as a nominalized form — 'a pre-judgment' — which is what préjugé means as a noun. French often uses past participles as nouns this way.

words.exampleSentence: Il faut lutter contre les préjugés dans notre société.
words.exampleTranslation: We must fight against prejudices in our society.

words.exampleSentence: Elle a toujours eu une attitude anticonformiste face aux traditions.
words.exampleTranslation: She has always had a nonconformist attitude toward traditions.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -iser (equivalent to English -ize) turns the noun 'humain/human' into a verb. Combined with dés-, the result is 'to dehumanize' — to strip of human qualities. The -er is the standard French infinitive ending.

words.translationLong: to dehumanize, to strip of humanity
words.exampleSentence: Certaines conditions de travail tendent à déshumaniser les employés.
words.exampleTranslation: Certain working conditions tend to dehumanize employees.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin recuperare (to get back, to regain). The core idea is retrieval or getting hold of something. Related to 'recuperate' in English.

words.exampleSentence: La récupération des données après la panne a pris plusieurs heures.
words.exampleTranslation: Recovering the data after the crash took several hours.

words.exampleSentence: Il attendait avec impatience les résultats de son examen.
words.exampleTranslation: He was waiting impatiently for his exam results.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin concipere (to take in, to conceive). The con- here is part of the root 'concevoir' rather than a freestanding prefix meaning 'together' — it fused long ago into the verb's core meaning.

words.translationLong: to form a preconceived idea of
words.exampleSentence: Il ne faut pas préconcevoir l'issue d'un procès avant d'entendre les témoins.
words.exampleTranslation: You shouldn't preconceive the outcome of a trial before hearing the witnesses.

words.exampleSentence: Les parents ont tendance à surprotéger leurs enfants face aux dangers.
words.exampleTranslation: Parents tend to overprotect their children from dangers.

words.exampleSentence: Son argumentation était totalement inconsistante et peu convaincante.
words.exampleTranslation: His argumentation was completely inconsistent and unconvincing.

words.translationLong: to disrupt, to throw off, to put out of order
words.exampleSentence: Ce changement d'heure a complètement dérèglé mon sommeil.
words.exampleTranslation: This time change has completely disrupted my sleep.

words.exampleSentence: La réactivation du volcan a surpris les scientifiques.
words.exampleTranslation: The reactivation of the volcano surprised scientists.

words.exampleSentence: Le changement était si imperceptible qu'on ne le remarquait pas.
words.exampleTranslation: The change was so imperceptible that no one noticed it.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin seligere (to pick out, to select). The sé- here is a reduced form of se- (apart), part of the root, not a freestanding prefix — it fused into 'sélection' long ago.

words.exampleSentence: La présélection des candidats a été effectuée par un jury indépendant.
words.exampleTranslation: The pre-selection of candidates was carried out by an independent jury.

words.translationLong: to disorient, to confuse, to bewilder
words.exampleSentence: Le labyrinthe était conçu pour désorienter les visiteurs.
words.exampleTranslation: The labyrinth was designed to disorient visitors.

words.exampleSentence: Il a tendance à surestimer ses propres capacités.
words.exampleTranslation: He tends to overestimate his own abilities.

words.exampleSentence: L'immobilisation du blessé était nécessaire avant le transport.
words.exampleTranslation: Immobilizing the injured person was necessary before transport.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin praeconizare (to proclaim publicly), derived from praeco (herald, crier). In modern French, préconiser means to recommend or advocate. The root itself already carried the idea of publicly urging something, so pré- reinforces the forward-looking, anticipatory sense.

words.exampleSentence: La préconisation du médecin était de réduire la consommation de sel.
words.exampleTranslation: The doctor's recommendation was to reduce salt intake.

words.exampleSentence: La désintégration de l'empire a pris plusieurs siècles.
words.exampleTranslation: The disintegration of the empire took several centuries.

words.exampleSentence: Elle a décidé de réinvestir ses bénéfices dans son entreprise.
words.exampleTranslation: She decided to reinvest her profits into her business.

parts.homeLangDetails: The -é here is part of the adjectival form derived from 'noyau/nucleus' via Latin nucleus. Together with -aire it forms the suffix that turns the root into an adjective.

words.exampleSentence: Le mouvement antinucléaire a organisé une grande manifestation.
words.exampleTranslation: The anti-nuclear movement organized a large demonstration.

parts.homeLangDetails: A linking vowel that smooths the transition from the adjective stem to the adverb suffix -ment. It carries no independent meaning.

words.exampleSentence: Le formulaire a été incomplètement rempli.
words.exampleTranslation: The form was incompletely filled out.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -ment turns a verb into a noun, similar to English '-ing' or '-ment' in words like 'financing'. Here préfinancer → préfinancement.

words.exampleSentence: Le préfinancement du projet a été approuvé par la banque.
words.exampleTranslation: The pre-financing of the project was approved by the bank.

words.exampleSentence: La surévaluation de l'euro pose des problèmes pour les exportateurs.
words.exampleTranslation: The overvaluation of the euro poses problems for exporters.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here dé- reverses or undoes the action — to de-responsibilize someone is to strip them of their sense of responsibility.
From Latin responsabilis — the stem that also gives us 'responsible' and 'responsibility'. The -abil- part corresponds to the English '-ible/-able' pattern.
Part of the -iser suffix that turns a noun or adjective into a verb, equivalent to English '-ize'. Together with the infinitive ending -er it forms the full suffix -iser.

words.translationLong: to relieve of responsibility, to make someone feel unaccountable
words.exampleSentence: Ce système risque de déresponsabiliser les employés.
words.exampleTranslation: This system risks making employees feel unaccountable.
words.flag: 5 parts — word is a layered derivation: dé + responsabil(ité) + is(er) + er

parts.homeLangDetails: Part of the Latin-derived -ateur suffix. The -at- element links the verb stem to the agent noun ending, just as '-at-' does in English words like 'organiz-at-ion'.

words.exampleSentence: Il est connu comme un grand réorganisateur d'entreprises en difficulté.
words.exampleTranslation: He is known as a great reorganizer of struggling companies.

parts.homeLangDetails: A variant of the prefix in-, meaning 'into' or 'inward'. Here it points to goods being brought into a country.

From Latin portare, this root means to carry and shows up in both French and English. Porter (to carry/wear), transport (transport), rapport (connection, literally carrying back). You already know this root from English porter, export, and import.

words.exampleSentence: L'importation de produits étrangers a augmenté cette année.
words.exampleTranslation: The importation of foreign products has increased this year.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin ex-, meaning 'out of' or 'outward'. Here it indicates goods being carried out of a country.

From Latin portare, this root means to carry and shows up in both French and English. Porter (to carry/wear), transport (transport), rapport (connection, literally carrying back). You already know this root from English porter, export, and import.

words.exampleSentence: L'exportation de vin français est très importante pour l'économie.
words.exampleTranslation: The exportation of French wine is very important for the economy.

parts.homeLangDetails: A variant of the prefix com-/con- (with, together), assimilated to 'col-' before an 'l'. It signals doing something jointly with others.
From Latin laborare (to work). The same root appears in English 'labor' and 'elaborate'.

words.exampleSentence: Ce projet est le résultat d'une collaboration entre deux équipes.
words.exampleTranslation: This project is the result of a collaboration between two teams.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin com- (with, together). Here it suggests sharing something mutually — making something common between people.
From Latin communicare (to share, to make common), related to munus (gift, duty, office). The idea is of making something common to all parties.

words.exampleSentence: La communication entre les équipes est essentielle au succès.
words.exampleTranslation: Communication between teams is essential to success.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin invitare (to invite, to welcome). This is the core root; the -ation suffix turns it into an abstract noun.

words.translationLong: an invitation, a request to attend
words.exampleSentence: J'ai reçu une invitation à la fête de Marie.
words.exampleTranslation: I received an invitation to Marie's party.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Greek organon (tool, instrument, organ). In this context it evokes the idea of structured, functioning parts working as a whole — like organs in a body.
A verbalizing element (from -iser, to make or cause to be). It bridges the noun root to the action suffix.

words.exampleSentence: L'organisation de la conférence a demandé beaucoup de travail.
words.exampleTranslation: The organization of the conference required a lot of work.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin re-, here meaning 'back' rather than 'again'. Réduction comes from Latin reducere — to lead back, to bring back down. The sense is of pulling something back to a smaller or simpler state.
From Latin ducere (to lead). Related to English 'duct', 'conduct', 'deduce'. Here it means bringing or leading something to a reduced state.

words.exampleSentence: Le magasin offre une réduction de vingt pour cent ce week-end.
words.exampleTranslation: The store is offering a twenty percent reduction this weekend.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin pro-, meaning 'forward' or 'forth'. In production, the idea is of bringing something forth — leading it out into existence.

words.exampleSentence: La production de cette usine a doublé en deux ans.
words.exampleTranslation: This factory's production has doubled in two years.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin prae- (before, in front of). In présentation, the root sense is placing something in front of others — bringing it forward to be seen.
From Latin praesens / praesentare (to place before, to present). Related to English 'present' (to place in front of). The root sentare relates to 'being there' or 'placing'.

words.exampleSentence: Sa présentation devant la classe était très claire et bien structurée.
words.exampleTranslation: Her presentation in front of the class was very clear and well structured.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin solvere (to loosen, to free, to dissolve). The idea is of untying a problem — freeing it from its knot. The same root gives English 'solve', 'dissolve', and 'absolve'.
A variant of the -tion suffix, appearing after roots ending in the Latin pattern -ut-. It functions the same way: turning a verb concept into an abstract noun.

words.exampleSentence: Nous cherchons une solution à ce problème depuis des semaines.
words.exampleTranslation: We have been looking for a solution to this problem for weeks.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin re-, here carrying both senses: a turning back and a turning around. A révolution is literally a full turn — completing a cycle back to a starting point, or overturning an established order.
From Latin volvere (to roll, to turn). Related to English 'revolve', 'evolve', 'involve'. The image is of something turning or rolling over.
A variant of the -tion suffix appearing after Latin -ut- stems. It functions identically to -tion, forming abstract nouns from verb concepts.

words.exampleSentence: La Révolution française a transformé la société européenne.
words.exampleTranslation: The French Revolution transformed European society.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin in-, here meaning 'into' or 'upon'. In institution, the sense is of setting something in place — establishing it firmly.
From Latin statuere/sistere (to set up, to place, to cause to stand). The same root appears in 'constitute', 'substitute', and 'statute'. Here it means placing or establishing something formally.
A variant of the -tion suffix appearing after Latin -ut- stems. It functions identically to -tion, forming abstract nouns from verb concepts.

words.exampleSentence: L'université est une institution importante pour la société.
words.exampleTranslation: The university is an important institution for society.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin per-, meaning 'through' or 'completely'. In permission, it conveys the idea of allowing something to pass through — letting it go forward without obstacle.
From Latin mittere (to send, to let go). The same root appears in 'mission', 'admit', 'submit', and 'transmit'. Here the idea is of sending something through — letting it happen.

words.exampleSentence: Il a demandé la permission de partir tôt.
words.exampleTranslation: He asked for permission to leave early.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin com- (with, together). Here it has an intensifying sense — grasping something completely, taking it all in at once.
From Latin prehendere (to grasp, to seize). Related to '-prendre' in French and to English 'prehensile'. The idea is of mentally seizing or holding a concept.

words.exampleSentence: La compréhension de ce texte demande une attention particulière.
words.exampleTranslation: Understanding this text requires particular attention.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin de-, here meaning 'off' or 'away from'. In décision, the image is of cutting away alternatives — separating one option from all the others by choosing it.
From Latin caedere/cisus (to cut). The same root appears in 'incision', 'scissors', and 'precise'. A decision is literally a cutting-off — the moment you sever yourself from other possibilities.

words.exampleSentence: C'est une décision difficile à prendre.
words.exampleTranslation: It is a difficult decision to make.

words.exampleSentence: La division du travail améliore l'efficacité.
words.exampleTranslation: The division of labor improves efficiency.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin ad-, meaning 'toward' or 'into.' Here it conveys movement or access into something — being let in.
From Latin mittere (to send). The same root appears in mission, permission, and transmission. Here the sense is 'sending in' or 'letting through.'

words.exampleSentence: Son admission à l'université l'a rendue très heureuse.
words.exampleTranslation: Her admission to the university made her very happy.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin ex-, meaning 'out' or 'outward.' Here it conveys pressing something outward — giving outward form to an inner thought or feeling.
From Latin premere (to press). The same root appears in impression, compression, and oppression. Expression literally means 'pressing out.'

words.exampleSentence: La liberté d'expression est un droit fondamental.
words.exampleTranslation: Freedom of expression is a fundamental right.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin pro-, meaning 'forward' or 'publicly.' Here it conveys a public declaration or avowal — professing something openly.
From Latin fateri/fessus (to confess, to declare). The same root appears in confession and professor (one who publicly declares expertise).

words.exampleSentence: Quelle est ta profession ?
words.exampleTranslation: What is your profession?

parts.homeLangDetails: Attached to feminine adjective forms to make adverbs, working exactly like English "-ly". Lentement (slowly), vraiment (truly), facilement (easily). Once you know the pattern, you can convert most French adjectives into adverbs on the spot.

words.exampleSentence: Elle a rapidement compris la situation.
words.exampleTranslation: She quickly understood the situation.

parts.homeLangDetails: The feminine form of the adjective lent (slow). French -ment adverbs are built on the feminine adjective form, so lent becomes lente before -ment.

Attached to feminine adjective forms to make adverbs, working exactly like English "-ly". Lentement (slowly), vraiment (truly), facilement (easily). Once you know the pattern, you can convert most French adjectives into adverbs on the spot.

words.exampleSentence: Il marchait lentement dans la rue.
words.exampleTranslation: He was walking slowly down the street.

parts.homeLangDetails: The feminine form of the adjective doux (soft, sweet, gentle). French -ment adverbs are built on the feminine adjective form, so doux becomes douce before -ment.

Attached to feminine adjective forms to make adverbs, working exactly like English "-ly". Lentement (slowly), vraiment (truly), facilement (easily). Once you know the pattern, you can convert most French adjectives into adverbs on the spot.

words.exampleSentence: Elle a doucement pris sa main.
words.exampleTranslation: She gently took his hand.

parts.homeLangDetails: Attached to feminine adjective forms to make adverbs, working exactly like English "-ly". Lentement (slowly), vraiment (truly), facilement (easily). Once you know the pattern, you can convert most French adjectives into adverbs on the spot.

words.exampleSentence: Je veux simplement être heureux.
words.exampleTranslation: I simply want to be happy.

parts.homeLangDetails: The feminine form of the adjective direct. French -ment adverbs are built on the feminine adjective form, so direct becomes directe before -ment.

words.exampleSentence: Allez directement à la gare.
words.exampleTranslation: Go directly to the train station.

parts.homeLangDetails: This is the feminine form of the suffix -el/-elle, equivalent to English -al (as in natural). The doubled -l- is standard in French before -ement in words like naturellement and normalement.

Attached to feminine adjective forms to make adverbs, working exactly like English "-ly". Lentement (slowly), vraiment (truly), facilement (easily). Once you know the pattern, you can convert most French adjectives into adverbs on the spot.

words.exampleSentence: Naturellement, elle a accepté l'invitation.
words.exampleTranslation: Naturally, she accepted the invitation.

parts.homeLangDetails: The feminine form of the adjective clair (clear, bright). French -ment adverbs are built on the feminine adjective form, so clair becomes claire before -ment.

Attached to feminine adjective forms to make adverbs, working exactly like English "-ly". Lentement (slowly), vraiment (truly), facilement (easily). Once you know the pattern, you can convert most French adjectives into adverbs on the spot.

words.exampleSentence: Il a clairement expliqué les règles du jeu.
words.exampleTranslation: He clearly explained the rules of the game.

words.exampleSentence: Elle apprend les langues facilement.
words.exampleTranslation: She learns languages easily.

words.exampleSentence: C'est exactement ce que je voulais dire.
words.exampleTranslation: That's exactly what I meant to say.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin re-, here carrying the sense of 'back' rather than 'again' — répondre literally means 'to pledge back,' from Latin respondere (to answer, to promise in return). Think of it as sending a response back to someone.
From Latin spondere (to pledge, to promise). This root also appears in correspondre (to correspond) and réponse (response/answer).

words.translationLong: to respond, to answer, to reply
words.exampleSentence: Elle n'a pas voulu répondre à sa question.
words.exampleTranslation: She didn't want to answer his question.

parts.homeLangDetails: Means doing something again or returning to a starting point, just like English "re-". It is one of the most productive prefixes in French: refaire (to redo), revenir (to come back), répondre (to respond). You will spot this one everywhere.

From Latin prehendere, to grasp or take. It lives inside comprendre (to understand, literally grasp together), apprendre (to learn), surprendre (to surprise), and entreprendre (to undertake). English surprise and entrepreneur both trace back to the same root.

words.translationLong: to take back, to resume, to retake, to take up again
words.exampleSentence: Elle a décidé de reprendre ses études après une pause.
words.exampleTranslation: She decided to resume her studies after a break.

parts.homeLangDetails: Means doing something again or returning to a starting point, just like English "re-". It is one of the most productive prefixes in French: refaire (to redo), revenir (to come back), répondre (to respond). You will spot this one everywhere.

From Latin venire, meaning to come. It sits inside devenir (to become), prévenir (to warn or prevent), revenir (to come back), and convenir (to suit or agree). Recognizing it helps you see that all these words involve movement toward something.

words.exampleSentence: Il va revenir demain matin.
words.exampleTranslation: He is going to come back tomorrow morning.

parts.homeLangDetails: Means doing something again or returning to a starting point, just like English "re-". It is one of the most productive prefixes in French: refaire (to redo), revenir (to come back), répondre (to respond). You will spot this one everywhere.

From Latin facere, meaning to do or make, this is one of the most central roots in French. Faire (to do/make), défaire (to undo), satisfaire (to satisfy), and it connects to English factory, fact, and perfect. It shifts form a lot, but the core idea of doing or making stays constant.

words.translationLong: to redo, to do again, to remake
words.exampleSentence: Tu dois refaire cet exercice, il y a trop d'erreurs.
words.exampleTranslation: You have to redo this exercise, there are too many mistakes.

parts.homeLangDetails: Means doing something again or returning to a starting point, just like English "re-". It is one of the most productive prefixes in French: refaire (to redo), revenir (to come back), répondre (to respond). You will spot this one everywhere.

From Latin videre, meaning to see. Voir means to see, prévoir is to foresee, revoir is to see again, and évident (evident) carries the same root. The connection to English vision, view, and video makes this root very learner-friendly.

words.translationLong: to see again, to review, to revise
words.exampleSentence: J'espère te revoir bientôt.
words.exampleTranslation: I hope to see you again soon.

parts.homeLangDetails: This prefix reverses or undoes an action, like English "de-" or "dis-". You see it constantly in everyday French: défaire (to undo), désaccord (disagreement), déplacer (to move/displace). If you know "de-" in English, you already have a head start.

words.translationLong: to discover, to uncover, to find out
words.exampleSentence: Les enfants adorent découvrir de nouveaux endroits.
words.exampleTranslation: Children love to discover new places.

parts.homeLangDetails: This prefix reverses or undoes an action, like English "de-" or "dis-". You see it constantly in everyday French: défaire (to undo), désaccord (disagreement), déplacer (to move/displace). If you know "de-" in English, you already have a head start.

words.translationLong: to move, to displace, to shift, to relocate
words.exampleSentence: Peux-tu déplacer cette chaise près de la fenêtre ?
words.exampleTranslation: Can you move this chair near the window?

parts.homeLangDetails: This prefix reverses or undoes an action, like English "de-" or "dis-". You see it constantly in everyday French: défaire (to undo), désaccord (disagreement), déplacer (to move/displace). If you know "de-" in English, you already have a head start.

From Latin facere, meaning to do or make, this is one of the most central roots in French. Faire (to do/make), défaire (to undo), satisfaire (to satisfy), and it connects to English factory, fact, and perfect. It shifts form a lot, but the core idea of doing or making stays constant.

words.translationLong: to undo, to unpack, to defeat
words.exampleSentence: Elle a défait sa valise dès son arrivée à l'hôtel.
words.exampleTranslation: She unpacked her suitcase as soon as she arrived at the hotel.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here dé- doesn't quite mean 'undo' in the usual sense. It comes from Latin de- meaning 'off' or 'down,' as in decidere (to cut off, to settle). The idea is cutting away uncertainty — arriving at a firm conclusion.
From Latin caedere (to cut). The same root appears in English words like 'incision' and 'precise.' In décider, the notion is cutting through alternatives to arrive at a choice.

words.translationLong: to decide, to determine, to resolve
words.exampleSentence: Nous devons décider où partir en vacances.
words.exampleTranslation: We need to decide where to go on vacation.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here dé- carries a sense of 'away from' rather than simply reversing an action — it comes from Latin de- meaning removal or displacement. Déporter means to carry someone away from their homeland by force.

words.exampleSentence: Des milliers de personnes ont été déportées pendant la guerre.
words.exampleTranslation: Thousands of people were deported during the war.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here im- is not the negating prefix (as in 'impossible'). It comes from Latin in- meaning 'into' — importing is carrying goods into a country. The spelling shifts from in- to im- before the 'p' of porter.

words.translationLong: to import; also: to matter (as in 'peu importe' — it doesn't matter)
words.exampleSentence: La France importe beaucoup de pétrole de l'étranger.
words.exampleTranslation: France imports a lot of oil from abroad.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin ex- meaning 'out of' or 'outward.' Exporter is the mirror image of importer — carrying goods out of a country rather than into it.

words.exampleSentence: Ce pays exporte principalement du vin et du fromage.
words.exampleTranslation: This country mainly exports wine and cheese.

parts.homeLangDetails: Means doing something again or returning to a starting point, just like English "re-". It is one of the most productive prefixes in French: refaire (to redo), revenir (to come back), répondre (to respond). You will spot this one everywhere.

words.translationLong: to postpone, to put off, to transfer, to carry back
words.exampleSentence: La réunion a été reportée à la semaine prochaine.
words.exampleTranslation: The meeting has been postponed to next week.

parts.homeLangDetails: A variant of Latin sub- (under), assimilated to 'sup-' before the 'p' of porter. The underlying idea is carrying something from below — bearing a weight or burden.

words.translationLong: to bear, to endure, to tolerate, to support (a load)
words.exampleSentence: Je ne supporte pas le bruit quand j'essaie de travailler.
words.exampleTranslation: I can't stand the noise when I'm trying to work.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin com- (together, with). Here it adds a sense of wholeness or completeness — comportement refers to how someone conducts themselves as a whole.

Here -ment is a noun-forming suffix, not the adverb marker. It turns the verb se comporter (to behave, to conduct oneself) into a noun meaning the manner or pattern of behavior. Compare gouvernement, where the same suffix works the same way.

words.exampleSentence: Son comportement en classe a beaucoup surpris ses professeurs.
words.exampleTranslation: His behavior in class greatly surprised his teachers.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin gubernare (to steer, to pilot), originally borrowed from Greek kubernetes (helmsman) — the same root that gives us 'cybernetics.' In French, gouverner means to govern or to steer.
A noun-forming suffix, not the adverb marker. It transforms the verb gouverner into a noun meaning the act or institution of governing. The -e- is a connecting vowel absorbed from the verb stem.

words.exampleSentence: Le gouvernement a annoncé de nouvelles mesures économiques.
words.exampleTranslation: The government announced new economic measures.

parts.homeLangDetails: From mouvoir (to move), related to Latin movere. The same root appears in émouvoir (to move emotionally) and mouvant (moving, shifting).
Here -ement forms a noun from a verb stem, indicating the action or result of moving. This is a noun-forming suffix, not the adverbial -ment in the glossary.

words.exampleSentence: Le mouvement des vagues était hypnotisant.
words.exampleTranslation: The movement of the waves was hypnotic.

parts.homeLangDetails: From changer (to change), itself from Old French, related to Latin cambiare. The same root appears in échanger (to exchange) and rechange (spare, replacement).
Here -ement forms a noun from a verb stem, indicating the action or result of changing. This is a noun-forming suffix, not the adverbial -ment in the glossary.

words.exampleSentence: Le changement climatique est un défi mondial.
words.exampleTranslation: Climate change is a global challenge.

parts.homeLangDetails: From établir (to establish), from Latin stabilire (to make stable). The same root appears in stable and instable.

This -iss- infix appears in many French verbs derived from Latin -ire verbs (like finir → finissons, choisir → choisissons). It extends the verb stem before suffixes and carries no independent meaning.

Here -ement forms a noun from a verb stem, indicating the action or result of establishing, or the place/institution that results. This is a noun-forming suffix, not the adverbial -ment in the glossary.

words.exampleSentence: Cet établissement scolaire accueille mille élèves.
words.exampleTranslation: This school establishment welcomes a thousand students.

parts.homeLangDetails: From investir (to invest), from Latin investire (to clothe, surround). In modern French it means to invest money or resources.
This -iss- infix appears in many French verbs derived from Latin -ire verbs. It extends the verb stem before suffixes and carries no independent meaning.
Here -ement forms a noun from a verb stem, indicating the action or result of investing. This is a noun-forming suffix, not the adverbial -ment in the glossary.

words.exampleSentence: Cet investissement dans l'éducation portera ses fruits.
words.exampleTranslation: This investment in education will bear fruit.

parts.homeLangDetails: From amuser (to amuse, to entertain), from Old French muser (to stare, to be idle). The prefix a- here is intensifying rather than negating.
Here -ement forms a noun from a verb stem, indicating the state or result of being amused. This is a noun-forming suffix, not the adverbial -ment in the glossary.

words.exampleSentence: Les enfants regardaient le spectacle avec amusement.
words.exampleTranslation: The children watched the show with amusement.

parts.homeLangDetails: From raison (reason, sense), from Latin rationem. The same root appears in raisonnable (reasonable) and déraisonnable (unreasonable).
The -n- here is part of the verb stem raisonner (to reason, to think through). The double-n in the spelling reflects this verb stem before the suffix.
Here -ement forms a noun from a verb stem, indicating the process or act of reasoning. This is a noun-forming suffix, not the adverbial -ment in the glossary.

words.exampleSentence: Son raisonnement était clair et convaincant.
words.exampleTranslation: His reasoning was clear and convincing.

parts.homeLangDetails: From traiter (to treat, to process, to deal with), from Latin tractare (to handle, to manage). The noun trait (feature, line) shares the root but traitement comes specifically from the verb traiter.
Here -ement forms a noun from a verb stem, indicating the action or result of treating or processing. This is a noun-forming suffix, not the adverbial -ment in the glossary.

words.exampleSentence: Le médecin a prescrit un nouveau traitement.
words.exampleTranslation: The doctor prescribed a new treatment.

parts.homeLangDetails: Turns nouns or adjectives into verbs meaning to put into a state or place, like English "en-" in "enclose" or "empower". Examples: emmener (to take away), entourer (to surround), emballer (to wrap/pack). The form is em- before b or p, just as in English.
From riche (rich, wealthy), from Frankish riki. The same root appears in richesse (wealth) and s'enrichir (to enrich oneself).
This -iss- infix appears in many French verbs derived from Latin -ire verbs (like enrichir → enrichissons). It extends the verb stem before suffixes and carries no independent meaning.
Here -ement forms a noun from a verb stem, indicating the process or result of enriching. This is a noun-forming suffix, not the adverbial -ment in the glossary.

words.exampleSentence: La lecture est une source d'enrichissement personnel.
words.exampleTranslation: Reading is a source of personal enrichment.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin producere (to lead forward, to bring forth). The -t- here is part of the Latin past participial stem produc-t-, from which French forms agent nouns directly. Related to produire (to produce) and produit (product).

Marks someone who does something, like English "-er" or "-or". Un chanteur is a singer, une coiffeuse is a hairdresser, un professeur is a professor. The -euse ending is the feminine form, so the suffix also tells you the speaker's gender.

words.exampleSentence: Ce producteur de vin est connu dans toute la région.
words.exampleTranslation: This wine producer is known throughout the region.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin dirigere (to guide, to direct), past participial stem direct-. Related to diriger (to manage, to direct) and direction.

words.exampleSentence: Le directeur de l'école a accueilli les nouveaux élèves.
words.exampleTranslation: The school principal welcomed the new students.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin invenire (to come upon, to discover), past participial stem invent-. The prefix in- here means 'upon/into' (not negation), and -vent- is from venire (to come). Related to inventer (to invent) and invention.

words.exampleSentence: Marie Curie était une inventeure brillante et pionnière.
words.exampleTranslation: Marie Curie was a brilliant and pioneering inventor.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin agere (to do, to act), past participial stem act-. The same root appears in action, actif (active), and réagir (to react).

Marks someone who does something, like English "-er" or "-or". Un chanteur is a singer, une coiffeuse is a hairdresser, un professeur is a professor. The -euse ending is the feminine form, so the suffix also tells you the speaker's gender.

words.exampleSentence: Cet acteur a joué dans de nombreux films célèbres.
words.exampleTranslation: This actor has appeared in many famous films.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin profiteri (to declare publicly), past participial stem profess-. The pro- means 'forth/publicly' and -fess- relates to speaking or declaring. A professeur is literally one who publicly declares or teaches knowledge.

Marks someone who does something, like English "-er" or "-or". Un chanteur is a singer, une coiffeuse is a hairdresser, un professeur is a professor. The -euse ending is the feminine form, so the suffix also tells you the speaker's gender.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin prae- (before). To prévenir is to come before something happens — either to warn someone in advance or to prevent something by getting there first.

The everyday verb for going, aller, has wildly irregular forms but extremely high frequency.
